assistant_message.content:
{
  "finding_id": "openscap_038",
  "safe": true,
  "verdict_reason": "Setting /etc/gshadow to mode 0000 is the default and required by the OpenSCAP rule. Root‑owned privileged tools (login, sshd, su, passwd, etc.) bypass normal POSIX checks, so authentication and related services continue to work. The change is minimal, reversible, and does not impact networking, firewalld, auditd, or other core services.",
  "side_effects": [
    "Non‑root scripts or tools that attempt to read /etc/gshadow will receive a permission‑denied error.",
    "Future package upgrades that recreate /etc/gshadow could reset permissions, re‑triggering the finding unless persisted."
  ],
  "services_affected": [
    "login",
    "sshd",
    "su",
    "passwd",
    "useradd",
    "shadow-utils"
  ],
  "recommendation": "Approve"
}
assistant_message.reasoning:
We need to evaluate safety. The remediation is to set /etc/gshadow to 0000. This is standard. It should not break services because root can read regardless of mode due to capability. Usually /etc/gshadow default mode is 0000 or 640? On RHEL, /etc/gshadow is mode 0000, owner root, group root. So the fix matches default. No break. Side effects: potential custom scripts reading non-root, but unlikely. So safe=true. Provide verdict_reason. List side_effects: non-root processes trying to read gshadow would fail, possible future package resets but not core. Services affected: none (maybe authentication but still works). Recommendation: Approve.

Return JSON with fields.
